summary: Most of us haven't experienced the 1918 influenza pandemic firsthand, and even those who lived through it were likely too young to remember. I think it's important to compare our current situation with that pandemic to understand the scale and impact of such events. The stories from health care workers during that time are invaluable for providing perspective on what it means to be on the front lines during a health crisis.

The COVID-19 pandemic has drastically changed both my work as a health care worker and as a researcher. I started practicing in the hospital in August 2020, right after the first wave began to calm down. Since then, everything has shifted—from wearing masks and PPE to adjusting research topics and methods to focus on COVID's impact, especially in low-resource settings. The pandemic has also affected how Duke University supports its staff and how we cope with stress and burnout.

I began practicing as a health care worker in the United States in August 2020, during the COVID-19 pandemic. Although I had experience working in Europe before the pandemic, my hospital work here started after the first wave had somewhat subsided. The US hospital environment required strict PPE protocols and health screenings, and the pandemic influenced how research and clinical work were conducted. Duke University, where I work, has made efforts to support health care workers during this challenging time.

Before the pandemic, I practiced as a health care worker in Europe for several years. However, I find it difficult to directly compare that experience with my current work in the United States during the COVID-19 pandemic. My research team is global, and working remotely has allowed us to connect with colleagues worldwide, including in Europe. This international perspective has helped shape how we approach the pandemic's impact on underserved populations.

When I first started seeing COVID patients around November 2020, the intensive care unit was a challenging environment due to PPE shortages. Nurses and physical therapists all needed PAPR machines and other protective gear, which were in limited supply. It took time for our department to have enough equipment so that we could safely care for patients without having to borrow from other units. The ICU experience highlighted the difficulties health care workers faced during the pandemic.

Duke University has been working to support its health care workers during the pandemic, although I sometimes felt caught between roles as a researcher and a supplemental physical therapist. They added three extra days of paid time off for health care workers to use as mental health breaks, which I appreciated and took advantage of. Duke has also started mental health workshops and sent out surveys to assess staff well-being, showing a growing awareness of burnout and stress among employees. While some support came a bit late, these efforts have been important for helping us cope.
